title: From Control To Surrender
study: A Study In The Life Of King David
scriptureForWeek: This week's texts will primarily be out of 1 Samuel chapters 13-16. It will cover the failure of Saul’s leadership and his rejection by God as king because of his unwillingness to obey God and desire to do things his own way.
weekTheme: Boys Control, Men Surrender God will judge our choices Have you ever met someone who just cannot let things go? They are super uptight and have to control every little detail. They micro-manage everything and everyone and don’t trust anyone else. Can you imagine how exhausting it would be to go through your whole life like that? Now imagine trying to control God like that. You tell him what he has to do, how he has to act, and what he has to accept. That kind of control would be even crazier. Well, this week you are going to see how bad the consequences are when you try and tell God how to do his job and take things into your own hands. Shift number 4 opens with the story of Saul, Israel’s first king. Saul will teach us the danger of rejecting God's leadership and taking things into your own hands. Saul mistakenly thought he could do things his own way and God would just have to accept it. But instead, God rejects him as king because he can no longer trust him. Jeremiah 17:5 says: This is what the LORD says: “Cursed are those who put their trust in mere humans, who rely on human strength and turn their hearts away from the LORD. This isn’t just a cute little verse; it’s a profound warning for a young man like yourself. You are not God. You cannot foolishly control your life. You have to learn to surrender to God's love and leadership or disaster awaits. This week, you will learn a couple of profound lessons you need to carry in your heart for the rest of your life, so let’s go.

days.title: Trust And Pressure
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 13:1-7 Saul was thirty years old when he became king, and he reigned for forty-two years. Saul selected 3,000 special troops from the army of Israel and sent the rest of the men home. He took 2,000 of the chosen men with him to Micmash and the hill country of Bethel. The other 1,000 went with Saul’s son Jonathan to Gibeah in the land of Benjamin. Soon after this, Jonathan attacked and defeated the garrison of Philistines at Geba. The news spread quickly among the Philistines. So Saul blew the ram’s horn throughout the land, saying, “Hebrews, hear this! Rise up in revolt!” All Israel heard the news that Saul had destroyed the Philistine garrison at Geba and that the Philistines now hated the Israelites more than ever. So the entire Israelite army was summoned to join Saul at Gilgal. The Philistines mustered a mighty army of 3,000 chariots, 6,000 charioteers, and as many warriors as the grains of sand on the seashore! They camped at Micmash east of Beth-aven. The men of Israel saw what a tight spot they were in; and because they were hard pressed by the enemy, they tried to hide in caves, thickets, rocks, holes, and cisterns. Some of them crossed the Jordan River and escaped into the land of Gad and Gilead.
days.reflection: Everyone has enemies in their life. In the Old Testament, one of the Israelite's major enemies was the Philistines. The Philistines were actually a loose confederation of five city‐states: Ashdod, Ashkelon, Ekron, Gaza, and Gath (weird names hey!) For the most part, these city‐ states waged war singly, but on occasion, they gathered together to fight the Israelites. And that is what is happening in today’s reading. A huge crew of Philistines has come to fight and destroy God's people. The Israelites realized they were in deep trouble. Look at what it says: The men of Israel saw what a tight spot they were in; and because they were hard pressed by the enemy, they tried to hide in caves, thickets, rocks, holes, and cisterns. Imagine standing in front of a huge army of people who hated you, were stronger than you, and were ready to kill you. It would be terrifying. So the Israelites hid anywhere they could. They seem to be outnumbered and outgunned and have no hope for victory on their own. They need God's help. They need God to step in and act. They need deliverance. God had given his people a king to help rule them. But the king had to trust God and follow his commands. He could not do whatever he wanted. He had to do what God wants. But this is easier said than done. There were going to be moments when what God wanted seemed a little crazy. God they would ask them to live by faith and not by sight and trust that he would come through And that theme of trust and surrender is going to frame the next 10 weeks. Which king is going to be faithful to God? Which king is going to surrender to his will, or freak out and take things into his own hands? You see, it’s under pressure that we learn what we really trust in. It’s when we feel like we are about to fail that we see where we look for help. King Saul had one approach, and King David had another. Saul tried to grasp power, and David learned to trust God. One king sought for control, another chose to surrender. You are going to face situations in your life that require you to humble yourself and trust in God. You will also feel the temptation to try and handle things yourself. You may feel pressure to lie to cover up a mistake rather than be honest. You may want to take things when your parents tell you to wait. You may think you can sin and get away with it, or cover things up yourself. But the truth always comes out. Adam and Eve refused to surrender to God. They sought to take control and be like God themselves. Sin entered the world, and disaster has been with us ever since. Jesus, however, chose to trust God. He did this even when they put him to death. The last thing he said on the cross was, “Father, into your hands I commit my spirit.” And as a result of his surrender, salvation entered our world. When you feel overwhelmed, what will you learn to do? To grasp control, or humbly surrender. Choose wisely. The consequences are huge, as you will see.
questions: On a scale of 1-10, (10 being the highest) how much of a control freak do you think you are? Have you seen any growth in yourself since starting The Primal Path?
What are some of the reasons guys your age can find it hard to trust God? Are there any areas right now you feel like it’s hard to trust God? You can be honest.

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a story of where they freaked out under pressure and tried to take ungodly control of things themselves. What was it, and what happened as a result?

thoughts.verse: I prayed to the LORD, and he answered me. He freed me from all my fears. Those who look to him for help will be radiant with joy; no shadow of shame will darken their faces. In my desperation I prayed, and the LORD listened; he saved me from all my troubles.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you learn to trust him. Ask him to fill you with his Spirit so you have more assurance of his power and love.

days.title: The Test Of Timing
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 13:5-14 The Philistines mustered a mighty army of 3,000 chariots, 6,000 charioteers, and as many warriors as the grains of sand on the seashore! They camped at Micmash east of Beth-aven. The men of Israel saw what a tight spot they were in; and because they were hard pressed by the enemy, they tried to hide in caves, thickets, rocks, holes, and cisterns. Some of them crossed the Jordan River and escaped into the land of Gad and Gilead. Meanwhile, Saul stayed at Gilgal, and his men were trembling with fear. Saul waited there seven days for Samuel, as Samuel had instructed him earlier, but Samuel still didn’t come. Saul realized that his troops were rapidly slipping away. So he demanded, “Bring me the burnt offering and the peace offerings!” And Saul sacrificed the burnt offering himself. Just as Saul was finishing with the burnt offering, Samuel arrived. Saul went out to meet and welcome him, but Samuel said, “What is this you have done?” Saul replied, “I saw my men scattering from me, and you didn’t arrive when you said you would, and the Philistines are at Micmash ready for battle. So I said, ‘The Philistines are ready to march against us at Gilgal, and I haven’t even asked for the LORD’s help!’ So I felt compelled to offer the burnt offering myself before you came.” “How foolish!” Samuel exclaimed. “You have not kept the command the LORD your God gave you. Had you kept it, the LORD would have established your kingdom over Israel forever. But now your kingdom must end, for the LORD has sought out a man after his own heart. The LORD has already appointed him to be the leader of his people, because you have not kept the LORD’s command.”
days.reflection: When you think of a fool, what is the first thing that comes into your mind? How do you think a fool thinks? What sort of decisions do you think fools make? Do you think anyone can act like a fool? Believe it or not, fools can be found in every level of life. Kids can be fools, and kings can be fools. Foolish people are not dumb. In fact, you can actually be really clever, even brilliant, and still be a fool. Being a fool is an attitude of your heart that rejects God and seeks to do things your own way. That’s why Psalm 14:1 says, “Only fools say in their hearts, 'There is no God.' They don’t say it in their mind; they say it in their hearts. That’s what happened in today’s reading. Saul is worried that because of the Philistine army, his people are going to run and scatter. Instead of waiting for God and trusting in his timing, Saul takes things into his own hands and oversteps his role as king. He offers a sacrifice that he is not allowed to, trying to get God’s blessing, without obeying God's word. When Samuel shows up, disaster happens. I felt compelled to offer the burnt offering myself before you came.” “How foolish!” Samuel exclaimed. “You have not kept the command the LORD your God gave you. Had you kept it, the LORD would have established your kingdom over Israel forever. But now your kingdom must end, for the LORD has sought out a man after his own heart. The LORD has already appointed him to be the leader of his people, because you have not kept the LORD’s command.” God is always looking for men he can trust. He wants to trust them with power, leadership, and blessing. But they must do things his way. When you take things into your hands, you take them out of God. It’s prideful to think that you know better than God. It's prideful to think that God will bless what you do when you refuse to do it the way he has commanded. Remember this verse 1 Peter 5:5-6? “God opposes the proud but shows favor to the humble.” Humble yourselves, therefore, under God’s mighty hand, that he may lift you up in due time. Saul learned the hardest way possible that trying to control God leads to disaster. In your life, you are going to find yourselves in situations where God's timing seems to be late. You may be tempted to rush God's timing around freedom and boundaries. Sneaking things ahead of when your parents think you are ready for them. You may be tempted to rush God's timing on sexuality, attending parties or school functions, or spending time with your friends. Nothing good will come from the rebellion of your own wisdom. God uses time to prepare us and get us ready for what he wants. Rushing things just wrecks the good plans he has in store.
questions: Can you name a time when you were younger when you did something that you can now see was foolish? What was it, and why did it seem like a good idea at the time?
What are a couple of foolish things guys your age tend to do? What possible long-term consequences do you think they may have?
Ask the person you are doing this study with how they learned to become wise and grow out of foolish decisions. Ask them to be specific.

thoughts.verse: There is a path before each person that seems right, but it ends in death. The wise are cautious and avoid danger; fools plunge ahead with reckless confidence. Proverbs 14:12 & 16 Read through his verse slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a heart for wisdom and hatred for foolishness.

days.title: The Test Of Obedience
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 15:1-23 One day Samuel said to Saul, “It was the LORD who told me to anoint you as king of his people, Israel. Now listen to this message from the LORD! This is what the LORD of Heaven’s Armies has declared: I have decided to settle accounts with the nation of Amalek for opposing Israel when they came from Egypt. Now go and completely destroy the entire Amalekite nation—men, women, children, babies, cattle, sheep, goats, camels, and donkeys.” So Saul mobilized his army at Telaim. There were 200,000 soldiers from Israel and 10,000 men from Judah. Then Saul and his army went to a town of the Amalekites and lay in wait in the valley. Saul sent this warning to the Kenites: “Move away from where the Amalekites live, or you will die with them. For you showed kindness to all the people of Israel when they came up from Egypt.” So the Kenites packed up and left. Then Saul slaughtered the Amalekites from Havilah all the way to Shur, east of Egypt. He captured Agag, the Amalekite king, but completely destroyed everyone else. Saul and his men spared Agag’s life and kept the best of the sheep and goats, the cattle, the fat calves, and the lambs—everything, in fact, that appealed to them. They destroyed only what was worthless or of poor quality. Then the LORD said to Samuel, “I am sorry that I ever made Saul king, for he has not been loyal to me and has refused to obey my command.” Samuel was so deeply moved when he heard this that he cried out to the LORD all night. Early the next morning Samuel went to find Saul. Someone told him, “Saul went to the town of Carmel to set up a monument to himself; then he went on to Gilgal.” When Samuel finally found him, Saul greeted him cheerfully. “May the LORD bless you,” he said. “I have carried out the LORD’s command!” “Then what is all the bleating of sheep and goats and the lowing of cattle I hear?” Samuel demanded. “It’s true that the army spared the best of the sheep, goats, and cattle,” Saul admitted. “But they are going to sacrifice them to the LORD your God. We have destroyed everything else.” Then Samuel said to Saul, “Stop! Listen to what the LORD told me last night!” “What did he tell you?” Saul asked. And Samuel told him, “Although you may think little of yourself, are you not the leader of the tribes of Israel? The LORD has anointed you king of Israel. And the LORD sent you on a mission and told you, ‘Go and completely destroy the sinners, the Amalekites, until they are all dead.’ Why haven’t you obeyed the LORD? Why did you rush for the plunder and do what was evil in the LORD’s sight?” “But I did obey the LORD,” Saul insisted. “I carried out the mission he gave me. I brought back King Agag, but I destroyed everyone else. Then my troops brought in the best of the sheep, goats, cattle, and plunder to sacrifice to the LORD your God in Gilgal.” But Samuel replied,“What is more pleasing to the LORD: your burnt offerings and sacrifices or your obedience to his voice? Listen! Obedience is better than sacrifice, and submission is better than offering the fat of rams. Rebellion is as sinful as witchcraft, and stubbornness as bad as worshiping idols. So because you have rejected the command of the LORD, he has rejected you as king.”
days.reflection: There is no such thing as partial obedience. You obey or you disobey. That’s the choice. Have you ever partially obeyed? Have your parents asked you to do something and you modified it to suit your liking? Has a teacher asked you to do something and you only did a bit of it and then made excuses? Partial obedience is actually disobedience. And the consequences of this in the sight of God are really heavy. God loves obedience. It’s not because he is a divine policeman trying to control us; it’s because he knows what’s best for us and seeks to protect us. Look at what Samuel says to Saul: Listen! Obedience is better than sacrifice, and submission is better than offering the fat of rams. But King Saul just couldn’t seem to get this. He felt like he knew better than God. He tried to control the terms of obedience that God had given him. He thought he could take things into his own hands and improve on what God had said. He kept the forbidden things that God wanted to be destroyed for himself and then tried to act like it was a good idea. God doesn’t want us to take matters into our own hands when he has given us clear instructions. God knows what he wants and why. Our calling is to obey. Joshua 22:5 says, “But be very careful to obey all the commands and the instructions that Moses gave to you. Love the LORD your God, walk in all his ways, obey his commands, hold firmly to him, and serve him with all your heart and all your soul.” Be careful to obey the details. Be careful to pay attention to exactly what God has said. Be careful to serve God from the heart. But Saul didn’t, and it was a disaster. God isn’t interested in you doing a bunch of religious things to try and keep him happy. He wants you to obey the things he put before you. Then the LORD said to Samuel, “I am sorry that I ever made Saul king, for he has not been loyal to me and has refused to obey my command.” God was grieved that he could not trust Saul. He was sorry that he put a man in power who was more loyal to himself than he was to God. God won’t tolerate men who try and control him. God won’t keep leaders in place who “partially obey” his commands. He wants wholehearted men. Loyal men. Men who trust and obey. Resolve in your heart to be one of those men.
questions: Can you think of a time in your past when you “partially obeyed?” What was it, and why did you choose to do that?
Why is simple obedience better than doing a bunch of religious good works?
Ask the person you are doing this study with about a time they partially obeyed. What was it, and what were the consequences?

thoughts.verse: “So remember this and keep it firmly in mind: The LORD is God both in heaven and on earth, and there is no other. If you obey all the decrees and commands I am giving you today, all will be well with you and your children. I am giving you these instructions so you will enjoy a long life in the land the LORD your God is giving you for all time.” Deuteronomy 4:39-40 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God for an undivided heart that wants to please him. Ask him for forgiveness for any areas of partial obedience in your life right now.

days.title: Rebellion And Witchcraft
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 15:10-23 Then the LORD said to Samuel, “I am sorry that I ever made Saul king, for he has not been loyal to me and has refused to obey my command.” Samuel was so deeply moved when he heard this that he cried out to the LORD all night. Early the next morning Samuel went to find Saul. Someone told him, “Saul went to the town of Carmel to set up a monument to himself; then he went on to Gilgal.” When Samuel finally found him, Saul greeted him cheerfully. “May the LORD bless you,” he said. “I have carried out the LORD’s command!” “Then what is all the bleating of sheep and goats and the lowing of cattle I hear?” Samuel demanded. “It’s true that the army spared the best of the sheep, goats, and cattle,” Saul admitted. “But they are going to sacrifice them to the LORD your God. We have destroyed everything else.” Then Samuel said to Saul, “Stop! Listen to what the LORD told me last night!” “What did he tell you?” Saul asked. And Samuel told him, “Although you may think little of yourself, are you not the leader of the tribes of Israel? The LORD has anointed you king of Israel. And the LORD sent you on a mission and told you, ‘Go and completely destroy the sinners, the Amalekites, until they are all dead.’ Why haven’t you obeyed the LORD? Why did you rush for the plunder and do what was evil in the LORD’s sight?” “But I did obey the LORD,” Saul insisted. “I carried out the mission he gave me. I brought back King Agag, but I destroyed everyone else. Then my troops brought in the best of the sheep, goats, cattle, and plunder to sacrifice to the LORD your God in Gilgal.” But Samuel replied, “What is more pleasing to the LORD: your burnt offerings and sacrifices or your obedience to his voice? Listen! Obedience is better than sacrifice, and submission is better than offering the fat of rams. Rebellion is as sinful as witchcraft, and stubbornness as bad as worshiping idols. So because you have rejected the command of the LORD, he has rejected you as king.”
days.reflection: I hope you are starting to see the big idea this week. Trying to control God and take things into your own hands never goes well. That’s because of how God views disobedience. Look at what it says in these verses: Rebellion is as sinful as witchcraft, and stubbornness as bad as worshiping idols. Whoa! Have you ever looked at disobedience like that? Like witchcraft or idolatry? It’s because disobedience is rebellion against a person and not just rebellion against a set of rules. God was grieved because Saul was actually rejecting him! Rebellion means: “Resistance to or defiance of any authority, control, or tradition.” Saul was establishing himself above God and rejecting his authority, even while sort of trying to do what God says. Partial obedience is not obedience. It’s a form of rebellion. Rebellion is as sinful as witchcraft, and stubbornness as bad as worshiping idols. Witchcraft may seem weird and wacky, like something from a Harry Potter movie. But it's not about spells and incantations and stuff like that. Witchcraft has 3 main goals. Domination, intimidation, manipulation. Read those again slowly and let them sink in. Trying to manipulate the circumstances to get what he wanted is exactly what Saul did. Dominating God's commands and placing himself over God's will is exactly what Saul did. Intimidating those around him to make sure he could do what he wanted was exactly what Saul did. Rebellion is as sinful as witchcraft, and stubbornness as bad as worshiping idols. Worshipping idols. This isn’t about bowing down to some statue in a temple in a distant country. Idolatry is looking to something other than God as your source of meaning, value, and worth. It's putting your trust and hope in something other than God. It can be yourself, acceptance by others, getting stuff you really want, or almost anything at all. Have you ever really analyzed what is happening in your heart when you disobey? If you really pick it apart, you will see these same themes inside you. Disobedience is always rooted in seeking to overthrow God's control in our lives. Disobedience is always about valuing something more than pleasing God. So when God tells you to obey him, do it! Trust him! He has your best interest at heart. Proverbs 3:7 puts it really well: “Don’t be impressed with your own wisdom. Instead, fear the LORD and turn away from evil.” I am so glad that Jesus was willing to surrender to his Father and not cling to control. Even though it was a struggle for him to do so in the Garden of Gethsemane, even though he was sweating blood, his surrender led to our salvation. As you grow, you are going to be around other guys who seem to manipulate authority and rebel against what God has said. You will feel these tensions on your own. Sin is never worth it. Pleasing God always is. God blesses those who obey him, but he removes the leaders he cannot trust. Ask God for a heart of surrender today!
questions: Rebellion against God is treated like something cool in our world today by the culture around us. What are some examples of this that you have noticed?

Are there areas in your life where you experience the temptation to rebel against what God wants? What are they, and would you be willing to share? (Just a note, guys face all kinds of temptations in their lives, you don’t need to feel any shame about this. Just be honest and bring things into the light so God shows you his grace and gives you power to deal with what you are facing.)
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how rebellion is both the same and different from when he grew up.

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Teach me your ways, O LORD, that I may live according to your truth! Grant me purity of heart, so that I may honor you. With all my heart I will praise you, O Lord my God. I will give glory to your name forever
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment and confess any areas you feel tempted to obey God or rebel against him. And take heart, he is merciful and kind and loves to renew and restore our hearts.

days.title: Losing The Kingdom
days.scripture: 1 SAMUEL 15:24-29 Then Saul admitted to Samuel, “Yes, I have sinned. I have disobeyed your instructions and the LORD’s command, for I was afraid of the people and did what they demanded. But now, please forgive my sin and come back with me so that I may worship the LORD.” But Samuel replied, “I will not go back with you! Since you have rejected the LORD’s command, he has rejected you as king of Israel.” As Samuel turned to go, Saul tried to hold him back and tore the hem of his robe. And Samuel said to him, “The LORD has torn the kingdom of Israel from you today and has given it to someone else—one who is better than you. And he who is the Glory of Israel will not lie, nor will he change his mind, for he is not human that he should change his mind!”
days.reflection: To control or to surrender. These are the choices that will define your life. Trying to control God as a person and turn his commands into our preferences NEVER goes well. Even though Saul is the king of Israel, the most powerful man of his time, it is all going to be taken away from him. Why? It’s actually quite simple. Saul tells us why: For I was afraid of the people and did what they demanded. Saul was more afraid of what his people thought than what God thought. Saul did what the people demanded over what God commanded. Saul wasn’t a man God could trust. He was a puppet of the people. Instead of leading his people to obey God, he became a slave to their opinions and tried to keep them happy. God's people would never flourish under a man who leads their hearts astray. “The LORD has torn the kingdom of Israel from you today and has given it to someone else—one who is better than you…” God needed a better leader. God needed someone with courage and conviction who was willing to stand up for the truth. A worshipper and a warrior. A man he could trust. Enter David. Now it’s important to note, David has no ideas about any of this at all. David is off looking after sheep. He is just a teenager like you, doing the job his dad gave him. But God is forming his heart when no one else can see. God is making David into a man who hates rebellion and idolatry. A man who loves the presence of God. God is working in David in secret, so he can use him in public. God is humbling David’s heart, so he can trust him with power. And believe it or not, God is preparing you to become a leader he can trust even now. That’s why you are being asked to do the Primal Path. That’s why there is this push toward becoming a godly man. So you don’t become like Saul. So you don’t lose your life and legacy and leadership when you are older. Your life matters. You are being asked to do this because you matter and you are loved. You may think of yourself as just a normal guy. Maybe someone who isn’t even a real man yet. But don’t overlook your growth and formation at this moment. God is working to make you a man who is loyal to him. A man who values God’s opinion more than other people's. Who doesn’t panic when there are problems. A man who surrenders all. A man who lays down the idol of control and takes a posture of surrender. These years of your life of some of THE MOST IMPORTANT in your whole life because you are establishing the patterns and habits that determine your destiny. You are guarding your heart so you can be a man of integrity. These years will determine the rest of your life. Saul’s kingdom is gone, and David’s has just begun. But it’s going to take a long time, and some real twists and turns before that works itself out. But for now, one thing is clear: Surrender is better than control. Just ask Saul.
questions: Why do you think having godly leaders is so important? Can you think of some examples in our world right now of ungodly men who are doing real damage?
How do you feel about the thought that God is working to form you into a man he can trust? How can you see him doing that, even in small ways in your life?
Ask the person you are doing this study with how they became a Christian. When did they give up control of their life and surrender it to Jesus?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. You have looked at them before, but now in light of what you know about control and surrender, read them again. Trust in the LORD with all your heart; do not depend on your own understanding. Seek his will in all you do, and he will show you which path to take. Don’t be impressed with your own wisdom. Instead, fear the LORD and turn away from evil. Then you will have healing for your body and strength for your bones.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to surrender your heart to Jesus. Lay your life before him and ask him to remove any of the stuff that King Saul did from your life.

extras: WEEK 1 MANSCHOOL ROCK CLIMBING MAN SCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: ROCK CLIMBING One of the best ways to learn to trust is when you rely on something to catch you when you fall. Learning to surrender to a safety harness and rope rather than free solo or cling to the side of a rock wall is a good, practical way to embody this lesson. And good for you too if you are willing to climb yourself. OPTION 2. WATCH HACKSAW RIDGE A true story about a man who has courage and conviction but refused to compromise. A WW2 hero story of the most aggressive and passionate passivist you can remember. A vision of how to be fruitful and faithful. https://youtu.be/RdjO0p4GJPA OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Take your son out to one of his favorite restaurants or for desserts and talk about the temptation of control and surrender. There is a fine balance between trust and passivity. Talk through the leadership failures of Saul and how that will try and manifest itself in your son's life.
